class_schedule.topic: Corporate vs Business Strategy and the Parenting Advantage
learning_objectives: Distinguish between business-level strategy (how to compete in a single industry) and corporate-level strategy (where to compete across multiple businesses)
Apply the Collis & Montgomery 'corporate strategy triangle' (resources, businesses, organization) to analyze multi-business firms
Evaluate whether a parent company adds or destroys value for its business units using the parenting advantage framework of Goold, Campbell & Alexander
Identify the 'better off' and 'ownership' tests from Porter's 1987 HBR article and apply them to a real conglomerate
readings: Collis & Montgomery, Corporate Strategy: A Resource-Based Approach (2nd ed., 2005), Ch. 1-2
Porter, 'From Competitive Advantage to Corporate Strategy' Harvard Business Review, May-June 1987
Goold, Campbell & Alexander, Corporate-Level Strategy: Creating Value in the Multibusiness Company (1994), Ch. 1-3
Grant, Contemporary Strategy Analysis (11th ed.), Ch. 12 - Diversification Strategy

lecture_notes.introduction: BA301 taught you how a single business competes: how Southwest Airlines chose a low-cost point-to-point model, how Apple wrapped premium hardware around a software ecosystem, how Walmart squeezed costs out of retail. This course asks a fundamentally different question. Once a firm owns multiple businesses, who decides whether those businesses belong together, and why should a corporate parent earn the right to exist at all? General Electric at its peak owned jet engines, light bulbs, MRI machines, NBC television, locomotives, and one of the largest financial services operations on the planet. Berkshire Hathaway today owns GEICO, Dairy Queen, BNSF Railway, Apple stock, and See's Candies. Disney owns theme parks, ESPN, Pixar, Marvel, Lucasfilm, Hulu, and a cruise line. What holds these portfolios together? When GE broke itself into three companies in 2021 after more than a century as an industrial conglomerate, the market cheered, arguing the parent had been destroying value, not creating it. This first session introduces the central puzzle of corporate strategy: a corporate parent must prove that the businesses under its roof are worth more together than they would be as independent companies. If not, the conglomerate discount is deserved and activists will demand a breakup.
key_concepts: Business strategy asks 'how do we win in this industry' and is the domain of BA301; corporate strategy asks 'what set of businesses should we be in and how do we add value across them' and is the domain of BA406

Collis & Montgomery corporate strategy triangle: the three vertices are Resources (what distinctive assets the firm owns), Businesses (which industries the firm participates in) and Organization (the structures, systems and processes that coordinate across businesses); all three must be aligned with a unifying Vision

Parenting advantage: the corporate headquarters adds value only if it is a better owner of each business unit than any realistic alternative parent; otherwise the business would be worth more under different ownership

Porter's three corporate strategy tests from the 1987 HBR article: the attractiveness test (the industry entered must be structurally attractive or becomes so), the cost-of-entry test (future profits must not be capitalized away at entry), and the better-off test (the new unit must gain competitive advantage from its link to the corporation, or vice versa)

Conglomerate discount: the sum-of-the-parts valuation gap, typically 10-15%, by which diversified firms trade below what their businesses would be worth as stand-alone entities, reflecting investor skepticism that the parent adds value

Four parent value-adding approaches (Goold, Campbell & Alexander): stand-alone influence (targets, capital, staffing for each unit), linkage influence (encouraging synergies), central functions and services, and corporate development (portfolio additions and subtractions)

Value destruction risks: the corporate parent can destroy value by imposing bureaucratic overhead, misallocating capital, mis-timing acquisitions at cycle peaks, or starving cash-generating units to subsidize politically favored ones

Heartland businesses vs alien territory: Goold et al. argue every corporate parent has a 'heartland' of businesses where its particular parenting style creates value and 'alien territory' where the same style destroys value; Disney's heartland is IP-driven entertainment, not theme parks in isolation

Corporate vs shareholder perspective: if diversification benefits only management (empire building, career risk reduction) while individual shareholders could achieve the same diversification themselves at lower cost through a portfolio of stocks, the corporate strategy is illegitimate

The three levels of strategy dialogue: corporate strategy (portfolio and parenting), business strategy (competitive positioning within one industry), and functional strategy (operational execution within functions like marketing or supply chain)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Work through a structured application of Porter's three tests to The Walt Disney Company's 2019 acquisition of 21st Century Fox assets for $71.3 billion. Attractiveness test: was the Fox film library and FX/Nat Geo cable portfolio in a structurally attractive position given cord cutting? Cost of entry test: did Disney pay a synergy-consuming premium, especially given the bidding war with Comcast that raised the price from $52.4B to $71.3B? Better-off test: could Disney uniquely extract value from Fox IP (X-Men integration with Marvel Cinematic Universe, Avatar sequels, Hulu consolidation, Disney+ content) in ways no other acquirer could? Compare to the Daimler-Chrysler merger of 1998, which failed all three tests and was unwound in 2007 at a $30 billion loss.
setup_requirements: Copies of Porter's 1987 HBR article 'From Competitive Advantage to Corporate Strategy'
Disney 10-K filings 2018-2023 for financial context on the Fox deal
Bloomberg or S&P Capital IQ access for sum-of-the-parts analyses of current conglomerates
Collis & Montgomery corporate strategy triangle worksheet

review.value: Corporate strategy begins with the question 'why should this parent own this business?' If you cannot articulate why the parent is a better owner than any realistic alternative, the business should probably be divested. This is the single most important question in the course

Passing the attractiveness test alone is not enough. If the cost of entry capitalizes away future profits, or if the new business gains no advantage from the parent, the move destroys shareholder value. A diversification move must pass all three tests simultaneously

Individual investors can hold a basket of stocks to achieve diversification at near-zero cost through index funds. A corporation cannot justify diversification on diversification-per-se grounds; it must justify on operational value the parent adds that shareholders cannot replicate in their own portfolios

review.key: Conglomerate Discount Is a Market Verdict
review.value: When a diversified firm trades below its sum-of-parts value, the market is explicitly telling the board the parent is destroying value. GE's break-up in 2021, United Technologies' split in 2020, and Johnson Controls' automotive spin-off in 2016 all came after sustained conglomerate discounts made the breakup inevitable

learning_objectives: Classify diversification moves as related-constrained, related-linked, unrelated, vertical, or geographic using the Rumelt typology
Explain the empirical finding that related diversification tends to outperform unrelated diversification on risk-adjusted returns
Analyze managerial motives for diversification that conflict with shareholder value (empire building, risk reduction, free cash flow absorption)
Apply the 'resources, businesses, organization' framework to judge the economic logic of a diversification move
readings: Rumelt, Strategy, Structure and Economic Performance (1974, reissued 1986) - the foundational empirical study of diversification
Grant, Contemporary Strategy Analysis (11th ed.), Ch. 12 sections on types of diversification
Palich, Cardinal & Miller, 'Curvilinearity in the Diversification-Performance Linkage' Strategic Management Journal (2000)
Markides, 'To Diversify or Not to Diversify' Harvard Business Review (Nov-Dec 1997)

lecture_notes.introduction: Not all diversification is equal. When Procter & Gamble added Gillette in 2005 for $57 billion, it added a razor business that shared P&G's core capabilities in consumer branding, mass retail distribution, and R&D around personal care chemistry. When Quaker Oats bought Snapple in 1994 for $1.7 billion and sold it in 1997 for $300 million, it added a business where Quaker's capabilities in warehouse-club distribution actively destroyed Snapple's independent-distributor DNA. The difference is not merely about 'related' versus 'unrelated'; it is about whether the resources the parent brings fit the requirements of the new business and whether the businesses reinforce each other's competitive position. Richard Rumelt's 1974 Harvard doctoral dissertation was the first large-scale empirical study to measure this: he classified Fortune 500 firms by diversification type and found that related-constrained firms, those that stayed close to a single dominant skill, consistently outperformed unrelated conglomerates on return on invested capital. Subsequent research confirmed a curvilinear pattern: moderate related diversification beats both pure-play and highly unrelated approaches. This session unpacks the taxonomy, the empirical evidence, and the agency-theoretic reasons managers may pursue diversification even when it destroys shareholder value.
key_concepts: Rumelt's diversification typology: Single Business (95%+ revenue from one business), Dominant Business (70-95% from one), Related-Constrained (under 70%, all units share a common core skill), Related-Linked (under 70%, businesses linked in chains but no single common core), Unrelated (under 70%, no meaningful operational linkages)

Specificity-to-firm test: a diversification move is 'related' only to the extent the resource being leveraged is specific to the firm and hard for rivals to copy; shared real estate is not meaningful relatedness, but a shared brand, proprietary technology, or channel relationship can be

Curvilinear diversification-performance relationship: pure single-business firms are exposed to industry shocks, highly diversified unrelated conglomerates suffer coordination and allocation costs, and moderately related diversifiers earn the best risk-adjusted returns on average
Operating synergies: cost savings or revenue enhancements from sharing physical or intangible resources across businesses such as shared R&D, shared distribution, cross-selling, and common purchasing

Financial synergies: the ability of an internal capital market to allocate cash flow from mature businesses to high-growth ones at lower frictional cost than external capital markets, justified by superior inside information (the hypothetical advantage most often claimed by conglomerates)

Managerial motives for value-destroying diversification: empire building (CEO compensation often scales with firm size rather than ROIC), employment risk reduction (a diversified firm is less likely to go bankrupt), and free cash flow absorption (mature cash-generators go shopping rather than returning capital)

Jensen's free cash flow theory: managers of cash-rich firms are tempted to waste cash on empire-building acquisitions rather than paying dividends or repurchasing shares; the solution is high debt, which disciplines managers by requiring interest payments

Diseconomies of scope: coordination costs rise faster than linearly as business units multiply, information asymmetries between units and headquarters worsen, and political battles for capital increase; past a threshold, the parent adds more cost than value

Diversification discount: empirical studies (Berger & Ofek 1995, Lang & Stulz 1994) find that U.S. diversified firms trade at an average 13-15% discount to the sum of their parts; the discount has narrowed since but remains real for poorly-run conglomerates

Rumelt's more recent verdict in Good Strategy Bad Strategy (2011): diversification works when it chains coherently to a genuine competitive advantage and fails when it is merely a collection of ambitions; 'bad strategy' often hides weak logic behind the word 'synergy'
lecture_notes.demonstration: Classify and evaluate three real diversification moves. First, Amazon's AWS launch in 2006: related-linked or unrelated? AWS leveraged Amazon's internal infrastructure expertise but entered a fundamentally different customer segment (enterprise IT); we conclude it was a resource-leveraging related-linked move that passed all three Porter tests because Amazon had proprietary operational knowledge no cloud competitor could match. Second, Philip Morris acquiring Kraft in 1988 for $12.9B and General Foods in 1985: unrelated in product but related in consumer-brand capabilities and retail shelf-space management; Kraft later spun out in 2007 when the reputational costs of tobacco linkage exceeded the synergies. Third, Sears buying Dean Witter and Coldwell Banker in 1981 to create the 'Sears Financial Network' selling stocks and insurance at appliance counters; unrelated, failed all three Porter tests, unwound by 1993.
setup_requirements: Rumelt typology classification worksheet
Financial data on three target firms for sum-of-parts analysis
Markides HBR 1997 checklist for diversification decisions
Access to 10-K segment reporting to classify conglomerates

review.key: Relatedness Must Be Operational, Not Cosmetic
review.value: Two businesses are only 'related' if a specific resource or capability actively flows between them in a way that improves competitive position. Both being consumer products, both using trucks, or both being located in Ohio are not meaningful relatedness. The test is whether removing the link would hurt either business

Berkshire Hathaway, Danaher, and Illinois Tool Works are unrelated diversifiers that outperform, but each has a distinctive and scarce parenting skill (Buffett's capital allocation, Danaher's operating system, ITW's 80/20 framework). Without such a skill, unrelated diversification almost always underperforms

review.key: Free Cash Flow Is Dangerous Without Discipline
review.value: Mature businesses that generate more cash than they can reinvest are tempted to acquire. Jensen's free cash flow theory explains why firms like 1980s RJR Nabisco launched massive diversification programs instead of returning capital, and why debt-financed LBOs were so profitable at disciplining them

The sum-of-parts valuation discount on conglomerates, often 13-15% in the U.S., is the market telling corporate boards that the parent is destroying value. Over time, this creates pressure for break-ups, spin-offs, or activist interventions. Boards that ignore it are eventually replaced

class_schedule.topic: Portfolio Management Frameworks: BCG and GE-McKinsey
learning_objectives: Construct and interpret a BCG growth-share matrix for a multi-business firm with explicit axis calibration
Apply the GE-McKinsey nine-box matrix using industry attractiveness and competitive position composite indices
Critique the limitations and misuses of portfolio frameworks (definitional ambiguity, experience curve assumptions, static analysis)
Make capital allocation recommendations grounded in portfolio position rather than intuition
readings: Henderson, 'The Product Portfolio' BCG Perspectives (1970) - the original BCG matrix article
Grant, Contemporary Strategy Analysis (11th ed.), Ch. 12 - sections on portfolio models
Ghemawat, 'Competition and Business Strategy in Historical Perspective' Business History Review (2002)
Collis & Montgomery, Corporate Strategy (2nd ed.), Ch. 4 - Resource-based view and its portfolio implications

lecture_notes.introduction: In the late 1960s, Bruce Henderson's Boston Consulting Group invented a visual tool that became the most famous artifact in the history of management consulting: the BCG growth-share matrix. Four quadrants, two axes (market growth rate, relative market share), and four labels (stars, cash cows, question marks, and dogs) gave corporate executives a one-page view of a sprawling portfolio. The BCG matrix rested on a sharp intellectual foundation: the experience curve, which held that unit costs fall by a predictable percentage with every doubling of cumulative production, so market share leaders should have structural cost advantages and generate superior cash flow. A cash cow's surplus should fund a question mark's share-building push until it became a star, which would eventually slow to cash cow status itself. This mechanistic logic fit the diversified conglomerates of the 1970s, where headquarters allocated capital among dozens of autonomous businesses. McKinsey and General Electric developed a more nuanced alternative, the nine-box matrix, which replaced BCG's single proxies with composite indices of industry attractiveness and competitive position. Both frameworks remain in use today despite withering criticism, because no alternative is as simple to communicate to a board. This session teaches you to build them correctly, interpret them honestly, and recognize when they lead you astray.
key_concepts: BCG growth-share matrix axes: horizontal axis is relative market share (your share divided by the largest competitor's share, with 1.0x as the threshold) and vertical axis is market growth rate (with 10% often used as the high/low threshold, though this must be calibrated to the industry context)

BCG quadrants: Stars (high share, high growth, reinvest to protect leadership), Cash Cows (high share, low growth, harvest and redeploy), Question Marks (low share, high growth, invest selectively or divest), Dogs (low share, low growth, divest or run for cash)

Experience curve foundation: unit costs fall 15-30% with each doubling of cumulative output due to learning, scale, and process improvement; this implies that the share leader has the lowest cost position and highest margin, making share leadership the strategic objective

BCG limitations: static (ignores time and strategic change), definitional (what market, what segment, what geography; changing definitions moves businesses across quadrants), narrow (two variables cannot capture strategic complexity), self-reinforcing (dog label can become self-fulfilling as funding is cut)

GE-McKinsey nine-box matrix axes: industry attractiveness (composite of market size, growth, profitability, competitive intensity, cyclicality, regulation, social and environmental factors) and competitive position (composite of market share, cost position, brand strength, technology, distribution, management quality)

Nine-box strategic prescriptions: three cells in the upper-left diagonal (high attractiveness, high position) get 'invest and grow', three diagonal cells get 'selectivity and earnings', and three lower-right cells (low attractiveness, weak position) get 'harvest and divest'

Composite index construction: each dimension weighted by importance (weights typically sum to 1.0) and scored 1-5; total score places the business on the axis; requires cross-functional input and is sensitive to weight assumptions

Modern portfolio frameworks: McKinsey's three horizons (current core, emerging businesses, future options), Ashridge parenting matrix (fit between parent skills and business needs), and real options thinking; all attempt to address BCG's static limitation
Capital allocation implication: portfolio tools support but do not replace business-unit strategy; a cash cow with a disruptor on the horizon may deserve defensive investment rather than harvest

Use and abuse in practice: the frameworks work when used as communication tools to surface assumptions and align the board; they fail when used as mechanical decision rules applied without business judgment
lecture_notes.demonstration: Construct a BCG matrix for PepsiCo's 2023 business portfolio. Segments (approximate 2023 data): Frito-Lay North America (revenue ~$25B, relative share vs #2 ~3.5x, growth 5%), PepsiCo Beverages North America (~$27B, relative share ~1.2x vs Coca-Cola in non-cola, growth 4%), Quaker Foods NA (~$3B, relative share ~1.8x, growth 2%), Latin America Foods (~$9B, growth 9%), Europe (~$13B, growth 3%). Plot each. Frito-Lay is clearly a cash cow verging on star. PBNA is a contested leader. Quaker is a cash cow. Latin America Foods is a star. Debate whether the axis thresholds chosen hide or reveal the strategic issues. Then construct the same portfolio in a GE-McKinsey nine-box with explicit attractiveness scores, and compare the two outputs.
setup_requirements: PepsiCo 10-K segment data 2022-2023
BCG and GE-McKinsey matrix templates with scoring sheets
Industry growth data from IBISWorld or Statista for matrix axes
Excel template for computing composite attractiveness and position scores

review.value: BCG and GE-McKinsey matrices are most valuable as board-level communication artifacts that force explicit comparison of businesses on common dimensions. They are least valuable as mechanical capital allocation rules that substitute for deep strategic analysis of each business

How you define 'market' dramatically changes the matrix. Is Tesla's relevant market all passenger vehicles (1% share), electric vehicles (18% share), or premium EVs (30%+ share)? The answer changes Tesla from dog to star to cash cow. Always make definitions explicit and test sensitivity to alternative definitions

BCG conflates industry growth with attractiveness, but fast-growing industries with easy entry, low switching costs, and powerful suppliers may be terribly unattractive. The GE matrix fixes this by making attractiveness a composite; in your analyses, always separate growth rate from full industry structural analysis

Treating cash cows as capital sources to milk for question marks systematically underinvests in defending mature profitable positions. Kodak's film business was a cash cow, until digital killed it faster than Kodak deployed the harvested capital. Today's cash cow may be tomorrow's dead dog if disruption is underestimated

learning_objectives: Define core competence per Hamel & Prahalad and distinguish it from mere technical capability or business unit strength
Apply the three tests of a core competence: customer value, competitive differentiation, extensibility to new markets
Use the core competence lens to identify diversification moves that leverage firm-specific resources versus those that do not
Critique the core competence framework's limitations: retrospective bias, definitional vagueness, and the lock-in problem
readings: Hamel & Prahalad, 'The Core Competence of the Corporation' Harvard Business Review, May-June 1990
Prahalad & Hamel, Competing for the Future (1994), Ch. 8-9
Leonard-Barton, 'Core Capabilities and Core Rigidities' Strategic Management Journal (Summer 1992) - the dark side of core competencies
Stalk, Evans & Shulman, 'Competing on Capabilities: The New Rules of Corporate Strategy' Harvard Business Review (Mar-Apr 1992)

lecture_notes.introduction: In 1990 C.K. Prahalad and Gary Hamel published what became the most cited corporate strategy article in HBR's history. 'The Core Competence of the Corporation' argued that the diversified firm should be understood not as a portfolio of businesses, but as a portfolio of competencies: bundles of skills and technologies that cut across business units and give the firm distinctive advantage in creating new products and entering new markets. They contrasted NEC, which saw itself as a learning organization with competencies in semiconductors, computing, and communications that would converge, with GTE, which saw itself as a holding company of separate telecommunications businesses. Over the next decade NEC successfully entered computing, mobile handsets, and semiconductors, while GTE's telecom businesses stagnated. Honda's competence in small engines lets it compete in motorcycles, cars, lawnmowers, power generators, and marine engines, all of which look like different businesses but rest on a single skill base. Canon's competence in precision optics and imaging lets it compete in cameras, copiers, laser printers, semiconductor steppers, and medical imaging. The core competence lens reframes the diversification decision: do not ask whether the new business 'fits' the existing businesses; ask whether it exercises and extends the firm's underlying competencies. This session formalizes the framework, applies it to classic cases, and then introduces its critics.
key_concepts: Core competence definition: a harmonized bundle of skills and technologies, not a single discrete skill; it is the collective learning in the organization, especially about how to coordinate diverse production skills and integrate multiple streams of technologies

Three tests of a core competence (Hamel & Prahalad 1990): provides potential access to a wide variety of markets, makes a significant contribution to perceived customer benefits, and is difficult for competitors to imitate because it is an integration of technologies and skills that took years to build

Core products: physical or intangible embodiments of core competencies that feed multiple end products; Honda's engines are core products, end products are motorcycles, cars, mowers; competition for core product share often matters more than end product share

Tyranny of the SBU: when strategic business units are managed as independent profit centers, no one has incentive to invest in the shared competencies that cut across them; core competencies atrophy as each SBU optimizes its own P&L at the expense of firm-wide learning

Competence vs capability: Hamel/Prahalad focus on technical and integrative skills (Honda's engines), while Stalk/Evans/Shulman's 'capabilities' framework focuses on business process excellence (Wal-Mart's cross-docking, Honda's dealer management); both reframe strategy around resources instead of products

Core rigidities (Leonard-Barton): the dark side of core competencies. The same capabilities that create advantage in current products can block adaptation to new ones. Kodak's film chemistry competence became a rigidity when digital arrived. Blockbuster's store-based distribution was a rigidity when Netflix shipped DVDs by mail, then streamed them

Competence-based diversification test: a new business passes the test if it draws on at least one core competence, contributes to strengthening that competence, and cannot easily be entered by rivals without similar competence

Resource-based view foundation: core competence is a special case of the broader resource-based view (Barney 1991, Wernerfelt 1984), which holds that sustained competitive advantage comes from firm-specific resources that are Valuable, Rare, Inimitable, and Non-substitutable (VRIN)

Retrospective bias: the Hamel/Prahalad framework is easy to apply after you know a firm succeeded, hard to apply predictively without risk of post-hoc rationalization. Discipline requires specifying competencies before observing outcomes, not after

Competence building as long-term investment: core competencies take 10-20 years to develop and require sustained investment through business cycles; short-term financial pressures can systematically starve them
lecture_notes.demonstration: Apply the core competence framework to Amazon's diversification moves. Starting competence bundle in 1995: e-commerce operations, long-tail inventory management, customer data analytics, and 1-Click purchase experience. Logistics expansion (AMZL, 2014-present): leveraged fulfillment and operations competence plus data on order density to enter a business UPS and FedEx had served for decades; passes all three Hamel-Prahalad tests. AWS launch (2006): leveraged internal infrastructure competence built to survive Black Friday traffic; market is different but competence is shared; passes tests. Amazon Basics private label (2009-present): leverages customer data and search placement competence to enter private-label CPG. Whole Foods acquisition (2017, $13.7B): does brick-and-mortar grocery leverage Amazon competencies, or is it an alien business requiring new competencies? Compare to Amazon's Fire Phone (2014) failure, which violated the framework because Amazon had no device hardware competence.
setup_requirements: Amazon 10-K filings 1997-2023 for financial timeline
Hamel & Prahalad HBR 1990 article
Core competence assessment worksheet with three-test checklist
Resource-based view VRIN framework reference

review.key: Firms Are Portfolios of Competencies, Not Just Businesses
review.value: The core competence view reframes corporate strategy: instead of asking 'what businesses are we in' you ask 'what skills and technologies do we uniquely master, and what businesses exercise those.' This question naturally surfaces both diversification opportunities and divestiture candidates where competencies have faded

A capability confined to one business unit is a business-level resource, not a corporate core competence. True core competencies cut across SBUs and require corporate coordination to invest in. This is why pure SBU-based organization destroys core competencies over time

review.key: Today's Competence Can Become Tomorrow's Rigidity
review.value: The same investments that created Kodak's dominance in film became the source of its blindness to digital. Core competencies require active management to avoid turning into core rigidities: constant testing, probing new markets, and challenging assumptions. Dynamic capabilities (Teece et al.) are competencies about adapting competencies

review.key: Diversification Passes When It Exercises a Competence
review.value: A diversification move creates value when it draws on a genuine core competence in a way that strengthens the competence while building advantage in the new business. A move that merely uses cash from one business to buy another, with no shared competence, is a portfolio move that shareholders could achieve themselves

class_schedule.topic: Vertical Integration and Transaction Cost Economics
learning_objectives: Apply Williamson's transaction cost economics to decide between making in-house and buying in the market
Identify the three core determinants of transaction costs: asset specificity, uncertainty, and frequency
Distinguish types of vertical integration: full, tapered, quasi (long-term contracts, equity stakes), and virtual
Analyze the strategic tradeoffs: control and protected margin versus flexibility, focus, and scale
readings: Williamson, Markets and Hierarchies: Analysis and Antitrust Implications (1975), Ch. 1-3
Williamson, The Economic Institutions of Capitalism (1985), Ch. 2-4
Grant, Contemporary Strategy Analysis (11th ed.), Ch. 13 - Vertical Integration and the Scope of the Firm
Coase, 'The Nature of the Firm' Economica (1937) - the foundational essay

lecture_notes.introduction: In 1937 Ronald Coase asked a question that had not been asked: if markets are so efficient, why do firms exist at all? Why doesn't every transaction happen between independent contractors? His answer, which won the Nobel Prize decades later, was that using the market has costs (search, negotiation, enforcement) and when those costs are high enough, organizing the activity inside a firm under hierarchical authority becomes cheaper. Oliver Williamson developed this insight into transaction cost economics, the dominant theory of vertical integration. Williamson argued that the critical variable is asset specificity: when a transaction requires investments that are worth much more inside the relationship than outside it, the parties become vulnerable to hold-up by the other side, and only internalizing the transaction (vertical integration) or writing expensive long-term contracts can solve the problem. Henry Ford's River Rouge complex in the 1920s was the archetypal integrated factory: iron ore arrived by Ford-owned ships, was smelted in Ford furnaces, cast into Ford parts, and assembled by Ford workers into Ford Model As. Today Tesla has revived the integrated model, building batteries at Gigafactory, writing its own chips, running its own stores, and generating its own training data for autonomous driving. Meanwhile Apple, which could vertically integrate into manufacturing, instead uses Foxconn. The TCE framework tells you why different answers can be right for different firms in different contexts.
key_concepts: Coase's insight: firms and markets are alternative mechanisms for coordinating economic activity, and the choice between them is an economic decision based on which has lower transaction costs for the particular transaction

Three Williamson determinants of transaction costs: asset specificity (how specialized are the investments required?), uncertainty (how hard is it to anticipate future contingencies?), and frequency (how often does the transaction occur?); high specificity, high uncertainty, and high frequency favor hierarchy over market

Asset specificity types: site specificity (plant located near buyer, e.g., power plant next to coal mine), physical asset specificity (equipment customized for a buyer), human asset specificity (skills learned in the relationship), dedicated assets (capacity built for one buyer), brand specificity, and temporal specificity

The hold-up problem: once a supplier makes relationship-specific investments, the buyer can threaten to walk unless the supplier accepts a lower price, and vice versa. Anticipation of hold-up leads to underinvestment unless the relationship is protected by integration, long-term contract, or hostage exchange

Bounded rationality and opportunism: Williamson's core behavioral assumptions are that humans are boundedly rational (cannot foresee all contingencies) and some are opportunistic (will exploit loopholes); together they mean complete contracts are impossible, so some governance mechanism must cover unforeseen events

Types of vertical integration: full backward (own the supplier), full forward (own the distribution), tapered (make some, buy some to maintain market discipline), quasi-vertical (long-term contracts, equity stakes, alliance structures), and virtual (tightly coordinated ecosystem without ownership)

Strategic benefits of integration: supply security for critical inputs, margin capture in the value chain, information flow between stages, and protection of proprietary technology or brand; examples: Tesla batteries, Apple custom silicon, Netflix original content

Strategic costs of integration: capital intensity (locks up capital across multiple stages), loss of scale if internal demand is smaller than an independent supplier serving many customers, loss of focus, and bureaucratic rigidity that insulates internal stages from market discipline

Make-or-buy decision tree: start with whether the input is strategic and whether specific investments are required; if both yes and the market has alternatives with hold-up risk, internalize; if neither, buy; mixed cases favor tapered integration or long-term contracts with hostages

Outsourcing wave of the 1990s-2010s: firms dramatically reduced vertical integration driven by falling coordination costs (IT), rising specialization benefits (contract manufacturers hit huge scale), and focus on core competencies; Apple's outsourcing of manufacturing to Foxconn, automakers outsourcing to Magna, Nike outsourcing to contract factories
lecture_notes.demonstration: Apply TCE to Tesla's vertical integration decisions. Battery cells (Gigafactory Nevada with Panasonic partnership, Gigafactory Texas 4680 cells in-house): highly specific (Tesla's cell chemistry and format differ from commodity cells), high uncertainty (battery technology evolves rapidly), and strategic (batteries are the largest cost component of an EV and determine range). TCE predicts full or quasi-integration; Tesla chose a partnership at Nevada and full integration at Texas as volumes grew. Compare to GM and Ford, which for 15 years outsourced batteries to LG and SK before pivoting to joint ventures (Ultium Cells, BlueOval SK) after hold-up and supply security concerns. Contrast with Apple's manufacturing: iPhone assembly requires specialized but not uniquely specific assets (Foxconn also makes phones for other clients), low hold-up risk given Apple's scale, and a decision to outsource that optimizes focus and scale. Contrast with Netflix's 2013 decision to produce original content: the input is highly specific, hold-up risk was real, and the strategic value of proprietary content justified billions in internal production investment.
setup_requirements: Tesla 10-K filings and investor presentations on Gigafactory economics
TCE decision tree worksheet with asset specificity, uncertainty, frequency scoring
Comparative data on vertical integration levels across auto OEMs
Williamson 1985 key excerpts on governance structures

review.key: Asset Specificity Is the Master Variable
review.value: Transaction cost economics predicts that the more specialized the investments required to support a transaction, the more likely it is to be internalized. When no specific investments are needed, markets work; when investments are highly specific, markets fail and hierarchy takes over. Everything else in TCE flows from this insight

review.key: Integration Buys Control at the Cost of Flexibility
review.value: Vertical integration gives you reliable supply and captured margin, but locks you into technology choices, scale decisions, and management attention that you cannot easily reverse. When technology is evolving quickly, flexibility often beats control, which is why Apple outsources manufacturing and why most chipmakers do not own fabs

Making some internally and buying some from the market lets you benchmark internal cost against market prices, maintain supplier relationships as options, and smooth demand volatility, while still capturing strategic benefits. Coca-Cola's bottling, auto engine plants, and many refiners all use tapered approaches

The right integration level is not a one-time decision. As technology, scale, and competitive context change, the calculus shifts. Firms that integrated in the 1990s mass outsourced in the 2000s, and are partly re-integrating in the 2020s as supply chain fragility, geopolitical risk, and AI capability concentration change the asset specificity calculations

learning_objectives: Distinguish horizontal integration from other growth strategies and identify its economic drivers: scale, scope, market power, and elimination of rivalry
Evaluate horizontal deals against antitrust standards under the Hart-Scott-Rodino framework and the Herfindahl-Hirschman Index
Analyze the tension between efficiency gains and market power concerns in horizontal mergers
Apply the analysis to contemporary horizontal concentration in tech, airlines, beer, and banking
readings: Grant, Contemporary Strategy Analysis (11th ed.), Ch. 12 sections on horizontal integration
Porter, Competitive Advantage (1985), Ch. 9 - Horizontal Strategy
DOJ/FTC Horizontal Merger Guidelines (2023 revision)
Stigler, 'The Economic Effects of the Antitrust Laws' Journal of Law and Economics (1966)

lecture_notes.introduction: Horizontal integration is the combination of firms operating at the same stage of the value chain in the same or related industries. It is the simplest and most common form of corporate growth and also the most scrutinized by antitrust authorities because it directly reduces the number of competitors. When United Airlines merged with Continental in 2010, four legacy U.S. carriers became three, and capacity discipline improved enough that the entire industry shifted from chronic losses to sustained profitability. When Facebook bought Instagram for $1 billion in 2012 and WhatsApp for $19 billion in 2014, it eliminated the two most credible horizontal threats to its advertising monopoly; a decade later the FTC is suing to unwind those deals. When Anheuser-Busch InBev consolidated the global beer industry through deals from Interbrew-Ambev (2004) through the $107 billion SABMiller deal in 2016, it achieved enormous scale and scope but triggered forced divestitures in every major market. The business logic of horizontal integration is clear: eliminate a rival, capture their share, merge operations for scale, reduce overhead, and gain pricing power. The antitrust logic is equally clear: reduced competition usually means higher prices for consumers. This session gives you the frameworks to evaluate both sides and predict what regulators will allow.
key_concepts: Economies of scale: per-unit cost falls as volume rises, driven by fixed cost spreading, learning effects, indivisibilities (e.g., a blast furnace has minimum efficient scale), and purchasing power; horizontal mergers can unlock scale where organic growth would take too long

Economies of scope: cost savings from producing multiple products together, such as using the same sales force, distribution network, or brand across products; horizontal mergers that combine adjacent product lines can capture scope benefits

Market power: the ability to raise price above competitive levels without losing all demand; horizontal mergers directly increase market power by reducing the number of independent competitors and may enable tacit or explicit coordination

Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI): sum of squared market shares (expressed as percentages) across all firms; HHI below 1500 is unconcentrated, 1500-2500 moderately concentrated, above 2500 highly concentrated; mergers that increase HHI by more than 200 points in already-concentrated markets trigger antitrust scrutiny

Hart-Scott-Rodino Act (1976): requires pre-merger notification and waiting periods for deals above thresholds (currently around $111M); gives DOJ or FTC the chance to investigate, demand divestitures, or sue to block before closing

Unilateral effects: the merging firms can profitably raise prices even without coordination, because losing sales to the merger partner is now internal rather than a rival's gain; this is the dominant current theory for blocking tech and consumer mergers

Coordinated effects: the reduced number of firms after a merger makes tacit collusion (price leadership, capacity discipline, geographic division) easier; the airline industry post-consolidation is a canonical example

Efficiency defense: merging firms argue that cost savings from combination outweigh market power concerns and will be passed to consumers; courts are skeptical unless savings are merger-specific, verifiable, and substantial
Failing firm defense: a firm that would otherwise exit the market can be acquired even if the resulting concentration is high, on the theory that the alternative is even less competition

Remedies: when a deal would otherwise be blocked, the parties can often close by divesting enough assets to restore competition; AB InBev divested MillerCoors to Molson Coors to close the SABMiller deal; Google divested parts of its ITA Software acquisition
lecture_notes.demonstration: Evaluate the proposed Kroger-Albertsons merger announced in October 2022 at $24.6 billion. Calculate pre-merger HHI in illustrative local markets: Denver has five chains with shares roughly Kroger/King Soopers 25%, Safeway/Albertsons 25%, Walmart 20%, Whole Foods 10%, others 20%; pre-HHI = 625+625+400+100+200 = 1950, post-merger combining Kroger and Albertsons = 2500+400+100+200 = 3200, a change of 1250 points. This far exceeds the 200-point trigger for concern. Discuss the parties' planned divestiture of 400 stores to C&S Wholesale Grocers as the remedy, and evaluate whether C&S is a credible operator or (as the FTC argued) a weak buyer that will fail and ultimately consolidate share back. Compare to past cases: Heinz-Beech-Nut (blocked in 2001 because only three baby food firms existed), Staples-Office Depot (blocked in 2016 for B2B contract customers), T-Mobile-Sprint (approved in 2020 with divestitures to Dish). The Kroger-Albertsons deal was ultimately blocked by a federal judge in December 2024.
setup_requirements: DOJ/FTC Horizontal Merger Guidelines (2023 revision)
HHI calculation worksheet with sample markets
Case reports on Kroger-Albertsons, T-Mobile-Sprint, Heinz-Beech-Nut
Industry concentration data from Census Bureau and IBISWorld

review.value: Antitrust authorities use the Herfindahl-Hirschman Index as their first-pass screen for horizontal mergers. Deals that push HHI above 2500 with a change of more than 200 points face strong scrutiny. Know how to calculate HHI for any proposed deal before it becomes public

Current antitrust theory focuses less on collusion and more on unilateral effects: can the merged firm profitably raise its own prices? This question depends on how close substitutes the merging products were. Facebook-Instagram was problematic because the two were the closest substitutes in social-photo sharing

Cost savings claimed in merger defense must be achievable only through the merger, not through organic improvements either firm could pursue independently. Vague synergy promises are discounted; verifiable plant consolidation savings carry weight

Blocking is not the only outcome. Divesting a subset of operations to a credible buyer can restore competition in problem markets while allowing the rest of the deal to close. The art is in choosing the right assets and the right buyer; AB InBev-SABMiller closed because MillerCoors went to Molson Coors, a credible competitor

class_schedule.topic: Mergers and Acquisitions I: Motives, Types, and Synergies
learning_objectives: Distinguish strategic, financial, and defensive motives for M&A and identify which create shareholder value on average
Compute basic deal economics: premium paid, earnings accretion/dilution, and the present value of announced synergies
Classify deal types: stock vs asset purchase, cash vs stock consideration, tender offer vs merger agreement
Apply a synergy valuation discipline to separate credible synergies from aspirational ones
readings: Grant, Contemporary Strategy Analysis (11th ed.), Ch. 15 - External Growth Strategies
Bruner, Applied Mergers and Acquisitions (2004), Ch. 3 and Ch. 11
Koller, Goedhart & Wessels, Valuation (7th ed.), Ch. 33 - M&A valuation
Sirower, The Synergy Trap (1997), Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: M&A is the most dramatic tool of corporate strategy. A decade of painful operational improvement can be matched in a single announcement; an entire business unit can be acquired in weeks when organic build would take a decade. In 2023, global M&A volume was approximately $3 trillion. Yet decades of academic research show that most acquisitions destroy value for the acquiring firm's shareholders, while delivering large premiums to the target's shareholders. Mark Sirower titled his 1997 book The Synergy Trap for a reason: the acquirer pays a premium today for synergies that, if they are realized at all, arrive slowly over years, and are discounted heavily. Meanwhile the target's shareholders lock in the premium immediately. Sirower's key equation is NPV = Synergies minus Premium; if the premium paid exceeds the present value of realized synergies, the deal destroys value for the acquirer. The typical deal shows positive combined returns (the pie grows) but most of the gain transfers to the target's owners. This session teaches you the taxonomy of deals, the typology of motives (some legitimate, many not), and the arithmetic of synergy valuation. Next session will cover the integration challenge that usually determines whether announced synergies become real synergies.
key_concepts: Strategic motives: gaining market share, entering new geography or segment, acquiring technology or talent, blocking a competitor, realizing cost synergies, extending a platform; credible when linked to a clear operational value creation story

Financial motives: deploying excess cash, changing capital structure, tax shields, accessing target's tax losses, financial engineering; credible when the acquirer has genuine capital allocation advantage (e.g., Berkshire Hathaway)

Defensive motives: preempting a rival, locking up a supplier, building a market position large enough to deter entry, gaining scale to match a consolidating customer; sometimes legitimate, often a rationalization for size-for-size's-sake

Managerial motives (problematic): empire building, hubris (Roll 1986), executive compensation tied to firm size, career risk reduction, response to peer pressure in an M&A wave; these motives destroy shareholder value

Deal structures: asset purchase (buyer acquires specific assets and liabilities, better tax treatment for buyer), stock purchase (buyer acquires target's equity, simpler but inherits all liabilities), statutory merger (two firms become one legal entity), and reverse merger (private firm goes public via acquisition of a shell)

Consideration: cash (target shareholders realize gains immediately, taxable), stock (target shareholders share upside/downside with acquirer, often tax-deferred), or a mix; all-stock deals signal acquirer confidence and preserve cash but dilute existing shareholders

Tender offer vs friendly merger: tender offer goes directly to target shareholders bypassing management, often used for hostile deals; friendly merger involves negotiated agreement with target board, easier to complete but slower
Premium paid: the purchase price minus the target's pre-announcement market cap divided by pre-announcement market cap; typical premiums range 20-40% but can exceed 70% in bidding wars

Accretion/dilution analysis: calculate pro forma EPS after the deal (combined earnings divided by new share count); deal is accretive if EPS rises, dilutive if it falls; accretion alone does not create value (it can come from buying a lower-P/E firm for stock) but dilution is a red flag

Synergy valuation: estimate annual synergy value (cost savings plus revenue synergies minus integration costs), apply appropriate discount rate, subtract present value from premium paid; if net positive, deal creates value for acquirer
Sirower's rule: NPV to acquirer equals present value of synergies minus premium paid; in most deals this is negative because synergies are overestimated and premiums overpaid
lecture_notes.demonstration: Work through the synergy arithmetic of Facebook's 2014 WhatsApp acquisition for $19 billion ($4B cash, $12B Facebook stock, $3B restricted stock). WhatsApp had about 450 million users, 55 employees, and minimal revenue. Pre-announcement implied valuation by secondary market trades was around $5 billion, so Facebook paid a roughly $14 billion premium. Where do $14 billion in present-value synergies come from? Thesis: WhatsApp's growth to 2 billion users was worth it as a defensive moat against Facebook's own users migrating to messaging, plus eventual monetization through business messaging and ads. Ten years later: WhatsApp has 2.4 billion users, business messaging is growing fast, and Meta's market cap has roughly tripled since the deal. Sirower math: if WhatsApp contributes $3 billion in annual run-rate profit by 2030 and grows, discounted at 10%, present value exceeds the premium. But this required a decade of patience and faced major execution and regulatory risk. Compare to Yahoo's Tumblr acquisition (2013, $1.1B, written down to zero by 2016) as the counter-example of undisciplined synergy assumptions.
setup_requirements: Bruner Applied M&A Ch. 11 synergy valuation templates
Accretion/dilution spreadsheet template
Historical deal data on premiums paid in recent tech deals
S&P Capital IQ access for pre-announcement market caps

review.value: The empirical record across decades of studies is consistent: around 50-70% of acquisitions destroy value for the acquirer's shareholders, while targets capture the premium. This is the base rate you should start from when evaluating any deal. The default assumption should be skepticism

Write down announced synergies before the deal closes. Specify cost savings with source, timeline, and probability; specify revenue synergies as incremental customers or ARPU with realism tests. Compare present value of synergies to the premium paid. If synergies are less than the premium, the deal destroys value and should be rejected

review.key: Premium Is a Tangible Cost, Synergies Are Hopes
review.value: The premium paid over market value is a hard cash commitment that transfers wealth to target shareholders. Synergies are probabilistic future benefits that often fail to materialize. Treat them with asymmetric skepticism: discount synergies heavily, treat the premium as a certain cost

review.key: Beware of Hubris and Deal Momentum
review.value: Richard Roll's 1986 hubris hypothesis argues that many deals happen because acquirer CEOs are overconfident about their ability to extract value. Deal momentum (the sunk cost of advisory fees, executive attention, and public announcements) then makes walking away psychologically difficult. Build walk-away triggers before negotiation

class_schedule.topic: Mergers and Acquisitions II: Integration, Culture, and Why Most Fail
learning_objectives: Design post-merger integration approaches (absorption, preservation, symbiosis, holding) based on strategic interdependence and autonomy needs
Identify the most common causes of M&A failure: culture, communication, pace mistakes, and key talent loss
Apply Haspeslagh & Jemison's acquisition integration framework to real deals
Analyze why the AOL-Time Warner merger failed despite passing formal strategic tests
readings: Haspeslagh & Jemison, Managing Acquisitions: Creating Value Through Corporate Renewal (1991), Ch. 6-8
Schweiger, M&A Integration (2002), Ch. 1-4
Bruner, Deals from Hell: M&A Lessons that Rise Above the Ashes (2005), Ch. 10-12 on AOL-Time Warner
Kaplan, 'Mergers and Acquisitions: A Financial Economics Perspective' Brookings Papers (2006)

lecture_notes.introduction: On January 10, 2000, AOL and Time Warner announced the largest merger in corporate history, with a combined value of approximately $165 billion in stock. It was hailed as the deal that would define the convergence era: AOL brought 23 million dial-up subscribers and a leading Internet brand, Time Warner brought film, television, magazines, and cable distribution. Nine years later, after writing down $99 billion of goodwill (by itself one of the largest write-downs in corporate history), Time Warner spun off AOL and the companies went their separate ways. What went wrong? The deal passed the strategic logic tests at the time: content plus distribution, shared ambition to be the first media conglomerate of the broadband age. It failed on integration. The cultures of the two firms were incompatible, executives from each side sabotaged counterparts, synergy promises were too specific too early, the dot-com bust deflated AOL's currency, and nobody could answer basic operating questions about who decided what. This session moves from deal economics to deal execution. It turns out the hardest part of M&A is not choosing the target or paying the right price. It is integrating two operating organizations after the deal closes. Most failures happen here, not in the boardroom.
key_concepts: Haspeslagh-Jemison integration framework: two dimensions, strategic interdependence (how much combined operations must share resources) and organizational autonomy (how much independent culture the target needs to preserve its value); four approaches follow

Absorption integration: high interdependence, low autonomy; fully merge operations, systems, and culture; appropriate for scale-driven deals (airlines, banks) where duplication elimination is the primary synergy source

Preservation integration: low interdependence, high autonomy; let target operate independently with financial oversight only; appropriate for platform or earnings acquisitions (Berkshire Hathaway's portfolio companies)

Symbiosis integration: high interdependence, high autonomy; gradual selective sharing of resources while preserving target's distinctive culture; the hardest to execute but essential when target's value depends on its people and culture (Disney-Pixar)
Holding company integration: low interdependence, low autonomy (rarely a distinct category); minimal oversight, often a prelude to spin-off or divestiture

Culture clash: when combining organizations with different values, work styles, decision-making norms, or risk tolerances, productivity falls, key talent leaves, and integration stalls; McKinsey research finds cultural issues are cited in 30-50% of failed deals

Key talent retention: target firms' value often resides in specific individuals whose tacit knowledge, customer relationships, and creativity cannot be replaced; without retention packages and explicit career paths, they leave within 18 months of close

Integration pace: moving too slowly lets uncertainty fester and key people leave; moving too quickly destroys the value of the target's distinctive capabilities; the right pace depends on integration type, with absorption fastest and symbiosis slowest

100-day plan: detailed integration playbook covering the first 100 days post-close, including leadership decisions, communication rhythms, quick wins, and milestone synergies; essential for maintaining momentum and credibility

Integration Management Office (IMO): dedicated team, usually led by an executive from the acquirer with target-firm representation, that coordinates workstreams, tracks synergy realization, manages communications, and resolves cross-functional issues

Communication overkill principle: in integration, there is no such thing as over-communicating; employees absorb maybe 10% of what is said and fill the rest with rumor; regular town halls, FAQ updates, manager cascades, and town halls are essential
lecture_notes.demonstration: Contrast two Disney acquisitions to show how integration approach determines outcome. Pixar (2006, $7.4 billion): Disney kept Pixar's Emeryville campus, leadership (John Lasseter, Ed Catmull), creative processes, and brand identity fully intact. Disney contributed marketing, distribution, merchandising, and the Disney brand halo, but did not intervene in Pixar's famous 'brain trust' creative review process. Nine years later Pixar had delivered consistent billion-dollar hits and Disney Animation itself had been revitalized by the same leadership. Classic symbiosis integration. Compare to Lucasfilm (2012, $4 billion): similar symbiosis approach, preserved Kathleen Kennedy as head of Lucasfilm, produced successful Star Wars sequel trilogy and Mandalorian. Now compare Disney's Fox acquisition (2019, $71.3 billion): much more absorption-oriented because the targets were cable networks and a film studio that Disney intended to merge into its own operations; culture clash, leadership departures, and less clearly value-creating outcomes. Then AOL-Time Warner (2000): attempted symbiosis but collapsed into absorption pressure and cultural hostility; $99 billion write-down by 2002.
setup_requirements: Haspeslagh-Jemison integration framework matrix
100-day integration plan template
Cultural assessment instrument for comparing two organizations
Case materials on AOL-Time Warner, Disney-Pixar, Daimler-Chrysler

review.key: Integration Approach Must Fit Strategic Logic
review.value: If the deal rationale is scale-driven cost synergies (banks, airlines), absorption is correct and speed matters. If the deal rationale is capability acquisition (creative content, R&D), symbiosis is correct and slowness preserves value. Choosing the wrong approach guarantees failure regardless of deal quality

The most thorough financial due diligence misses what determines outcomes: whether the two cultures can work together. Cultural assessment should happen before signing, not after closing. If cultures are incompatible and the deal requires deep integration, walk away

review.key: Retain Key People Before They Decide to Leave
review.value: Target-firm talent will evaluate the new world in the first 30-90 days. Offer retention packages tied to specific milestones, articulate clear career paths, preserve the most valued culture elements, and publicly commit to the target's leaders. Waiting to see who stays is too late; the most valuable people are already recruited elsewhere

review.key: The Value Is Won or Lost in 100 Days
review.value: M&A integration lore holds that 60-70% of the ultimate synergy capture is determined in the first 100 days, because early decisions cascade through the organization. Build a detailed 100-day plan before the deal closes, and hold leaders accountable for hitting milestones

learning_objectives: Distinguish equity joint ventures, contractual alliances, licensing deals, and consortia, and match each to strategic contexts
Apply Doz & Hamel's framework for alliance design: co-option, co-specialization, learning
Identify the most common causes of alliance failure and design governance to mitigate them
Evaluate real alliances for partner fit, value capture, and exit provisions
readings: Doz & Hamel, Alliance Advantage: The Art of Creating Value Through Partnering (1998), Ch. 1-4
Dyer, Kale & Singh, 'When to Ally and When to Acquire' Harvard Business Review (July-Aug 2004)
Gulati, Managing Network Resources (2007), Ch. 3 and 6
Grant, Contemporary Strategy Analysis (11th ed.), Ch. 15 sections on alliances

lecture_notes.introduction: Not every strategic move needs to be ownership. When Starbucks wanted to enter the Indian coffee market in 2012, it formed a 50/50 joint venture with Tata Global Beverages rather than going alone. When Sony and Ericsson combined mobile phone operations in 2001, they formed Sony Ericsson as an equal partnership (Sony bought out Ericsson's stake in 2012). When Fuji Xerox operated Xerox products in Japan for over half a century, it did so through a joint venture that became more successful than either parent alone. Strategic alliances, from simple supply contracts to full equity joint ventures, let firms access capabilities, markets, or risks without the cost and irreversibility of acquisition. They are most useful when the strategic need is bounded in time or scope (a specific market, a specific technology window), when antitrust prevents outright acquisition, when cultural distance makes absorption dangerous, or when the benefits can be captured without full ownership. But alliances have their own failure modes. Partners may compete with each other in other markets, may diverge on strategy or investment pace, may learn from each other asymmetrically (one party's intended skills transfer becomes the other party's capability leakage), or may simply fail to coordinate across cultures. Roughly half of alliances end within five years, often acrimoniously. This session teaches you when to ally, how to structure, and how to exit cleanly.
key_concepts: Types of alliance governance: equity JV (new legal entity jointly owned), minority equity investment (one partner takes a stake in the other), contractual alliance (binding contract without equity), consortia (multiple parties for a shared infrastructure project), franchising and licensing

Co-option logic (Doz & Hamel): ally to neutralize a potential competitor or bring them inside the tent, as Airbus consortium members did in European aerospace; appropriate when the alternative is destructive competition

Co-specialization logic: each partner brings complementary capabilities neither has alone (e.g., Starbucks contributes brand and coffee expertise, Tata contributes local distribution and real estate in India); appropriate when capabilities cannot be acquired quickly

Learning logic: one partner primarily wants to access the other's tacit knowledge; most risky because learning is hard to bound and the learner often exits once knowledge is internalized (Japanese auto makers' alliances with U.S. partners in the 1980s)

Alliance vs acquisition decision (Dyer, Kale & Singh 2004): acquire when synergies are hard (high interdependence and clear boundaries), ally when synergies are soft (capability access, market entry, uncertain outcomes) or antitrust blocks acquisition

Governance design: clear decision rights (what requires joint approval vs one partner's discretion), dispute resolution (escalation ladder, arbitration), capital commitments, exit rights, and non-compete provisions

Key alliance pitfalls: partner competition in other markets (learning-race dynamics), asymmetric commitment (one partner more invested than the other), cultural friction (national or organizational), and scope creep as parties push into each other's domains

Racing to learn: when each partner is trying to absorb the other's capabilities faster than they are giving their own away, the alliance becomes a learning race; the loser often ends up weaker than before the alliance started

Exit provisions: call/put options, buy-sell agreements, predetermined valuation formulas, and tag-along/drag-along rights; thinking about exit before signing is essential because most alliances end and doing it badly is painful

Alliance portfolios: many large firms manage dozens or hundreds of alliances simultaneously (Cisco, IBM); treating them as a portfolio with consistent governance standards captures more value than managing each as a one-off
lecture_notes.demonstration: Compare three real joint ventures. Sony-Ericsson (2001-2012): 50/50 mobile handset JV created to pool Sony's consumer electronics brand with Ericsson's telecom technology. Succeeded through the feature-phone era peaking with Walkman and Cybershot phones, then was crushed by Apple and Samsung; Sony bought out Ericsson's 50% for €1.05 billion in 2012. Starbucks-Tata Alliance (2012-present): 50/50 JV forming Tata Starbucks in India; Starbucks provides brand, roasting, training, Tata provides real estate, local operations, and tea sourcing for U.S. Teavana; still operating with over 300 stores in 2023. Fuji Xerox (1962-2021): originally 50/50 JV between Xerox and Fuji Photo Film for Japan and Asia; JV became more innovative than Xerox itself, pioneering business solutions; in 2021 Fujifilm acquired Xerox's 25% residual stake after legal disputes. Discuss what each alliance did right and wrong on co-specialization, learning protection, and exit provisions.
setup_requirements: Doz-Hamel alliance design framework
Dyer-Kale-Singh HBR 2004 ally-vs-acquire decision tool
Case materials on Sony-Ericsson, Starbucks-Tata, Fuji Xerox
Alliance contract term sheet template with exit and governance provisions

review.value: Most alliances are bounded in purpose and time. Unlike acquisitions, where the question is how to integrate forever, alliances should begin with clarity about what success looks like, how long the alliance should last, and how it will end. Planning the exit at the beginning makes the exit cleaner later

The ally-or-acquire choice comes down to whether the needed capability can be owned without killing it. Creative cultures, local market knowledge in distant geographies, and regulated joint infrastructure cannot always be acquired and integrated; alliances are the only path. When integration is feasible, acquisition usually captures more value

When both partners are trying to learn each other's capabilities, the faster learner exits stronger and the slower learner exits weaker. Define scope tightly, embed non-competes, and monitor what knowledge transfers each way. If you cannot protect your knowledge, do not ally

review.key: Governance Matters More Than the Letter of the Agreement
review.value: Alliances succeed or fail based on ongoing decision rhythms, trust between executives, and joint problem-solving, not contract language. Invest in the relationship infrastructure: regular steering committees, executive sponsor pairs, shared scorecards, and rituals that build trust. The contract is the foundation, not the building

class_schedule.topic: International Corporate Strategy and Global Reach
learning_objectives: Apply the integration-responsiveness framework (Bartlett & Ghoshal) to classify international strategies as global, multidomestic, international, or transnational
Evaluate entry mode choices (export, licensing, JV, wholly-owned subsidiary) against the CAGE distance framework
Analyze liability of foreignness and the resources that offset it
Diagnose failed international expansions and propose corrective action
readings: Bartlett & Ghoshal, Managing Across Borders (2nd ed., 2002), Ch. 2-4
Ghemawat, Redefining Global Strategy (2007), Ch. 2 and 4 - CAGE Distance Framework
Prahalad & Doz, The Multinational Mission (1987), Ch. 1-3
Rugman & Verbeke, 'A Perspective on Regional and Global Strategies of Multinational Enterprises' Journal of International Business Studies (2004)

lecture_notes.introduction: When Walmart entered Germany in 1998 through the acquisition of Wertkauf and Interspar, it brought its U.S. supercenter playbook: greeters, 'everyday low prices', centralized purchasing, and big-box footprints. Eight years later, having lost a reported $1 billion, Walmart exited Germany, selling its stores to Metro. Germans did not want greeters, expected fresh bakeries and butcher counters Walmart could not efficiently operate, and shopped at urban locations where Walmart's big-box model did not fit zoning. What worked in Arkansas did not work in Munich. International expansion is where corporate strategy confronts the hardest diversification problem: not just different industries but different institutions, cultures, consumer preferences, and regulatory environments. Yet the rewards are enormous: Nestle earns over 95% of its revenue outside its home country, Toyota sells more cars abroad than in Japan, and Unilever operates in more than 190 countries. The central challenge for any international strategy is balancing the pressure for global integration (one efficient global operating model, shared brands, coordinated R&D) against the pressure for local responsiveness (adapting products, channels, and management to each country). This session teaches the frameworks to make this tradeoff explicit, the modes of international entry, and the resources firms need to overcome the liability of foreignness.
key_concepts: Integration-responsiveness framework (Bartlett & Ghoshal 1989): two pressures — global integration (efficiency, scale, standardization) and local responsiveness (adaptation, sensitivity to local needs); four archetypal strategies follow

Global strategy: high integration, low responsiveness; standardized products and centralized decisions; appropriate for products where customer preferences are universal and scale advantages are large (semiconductors, commercial aircraft)

Multidomestic strategy: low integration, high responsiveness; country managers have broad autonomy to adapt products, marketing, and operations; appropriate when local tastes or regulations vary dramatically (food, personal care)

International strategy: moderate integration, moderate responsiveness; home-country R&D exported to foreign subsidiaries with limited local adaptation; simpler but often unstable; often an intermediate stop on the way to multidomestic or global

Transnational strategy: high integration AND high responsiveness; the 'best of both worlds' aspiration where firms achieve global scale while adapting to local needs through integrated networks; hardest to execute, requires sophisticated coordination

Ghemawat CAGE distance framework: cultural distance (language, religion, values), administrative distance (shared history, legal framework, currency), geographic distance (physical separation, time zones, climate), and economic distance (wealth, infrastructure, costs); total distance predicts difficulty of market entry

Entry modes on a commitment-control spectrum: exporting (low commitment, low control, lowest risk), licensing (low commitment, medium control via contract), joint venture (medium commitment, shared control), wholly-owned subsidiary greenfield (high commitment, full control), acquisition (high commitment, full control but integration complexity)

Liability of foreignness: foreign entrants face costs local incumbents do not (unfamiliarity, regulatory barriers, lack of trust, harder talent acquisition); to succeed a foreign entrant must have firm-specific advantages large enough to offset the liability

Ownership-Location-Internalization (OLI) paradigm (Dunning): firms engage in FDI when they possess ownership advantages (firm-specific resources) that can be profitably exploited in a foreign Location, and when internalizing the activity is better than licensing it

Regional vs global multinationals (Rugman & Verbeke 2004): despite 'global' rhetoric, most multinationals are actually regional (North America or Europe or Asia), with less than 5% of Fortune 500 firms earning balanced revenue across all three regions; global is harder than it looks
lecture_notes.demonstration: Diagnose two failures and one success using Bartlett-Ghoshal and CAGE. Walmart in Germany (1998-2006): Walmart used a global strategy (standardized everyday-low-prices supercenter model) in a market that required multidomestic adaptation. CAGE distance from U.S. to Germany: low economic and administrative, moderate cultural (consumer preferences, labor relations) and geographic; the moderate cultural distance was enough to break a model built on U.S. shopping habits. Walmart exited with a $1B loss. Tesco in the United States as 'Fresh & Easy' (2007-2013): assumed a global model of small-format neighborhood stores would translate, launched 200 stores, lost $1.8 billion before exit. Contrast McDonald's international: multidomestic on menus (McSpicy in India, McArabia in Saudi Arabia, Teriyaki Burger in Japan, wine in France) with global integration on operations, supply chain, and brand; over 60% of revenue now comes from outside the U.S.
setup_requirements: Bartlett-Ghoshal IR grid worksheet
Ghemawat CAGE distance calculator
OLI entry mode decision tool
Case materials on Walmart Germany, Tesco Fresh & Easy, McDonald's international

review.value: Before choosing global vs multidomestic vs transnational, diagnose the pressures your product category actually faces. Semiconductors face high integration pressure and low responsiveness; food and beverages face the opposite. Getting this diagnosis wrong leads to Walmart-in-Germany mistakes where a global model is imposed on a multidomestic context

Firms typically focus on geographic and economic distance but underweight cultural and administrative distance, which often matter more. A German firm expanding to Austria faces low distance on all four dimensions; a U.S. firm expanding to Japan faces high cultural and administrative distance even though economic distance is low. Score all four before committing

review.key: Liability of Foreignness Demands Firm-Specific Advantages
review.value: Local incumbents have built-in advantages: networks, trust, regulatory expertise, lower talent costs. A foreign entrant must bring something local firms cannot easily match (unique technology, proprietary brand, scale economies, superior management) large enough to overcome the liability. If you cannot articulate that advantage clearly, do not enter

Rugman-Verbeke's finding that less than 5% of Fortune 500 firms are truly balanced globally suggests that 'global strategy' is often oversold. A disciplined regional focus (dominate North America, then Europe, then Asia sequentially) usually beats simultaneous global expansion that spreads resources too thin

class_schedule.topic: Corporate Governance: Boards, Executive Compensation, and Activism
learning_objectives: Analyze the principal-agent problem between shareholders and managers and the governance mechanisms that address it
Evaluate executive compensation structures: base, bonus, stock options, restricted stock, performance shares
Assess board effectiveness: composition, independence, committees, and evaluation
Explain how activist investors target underperforming multi-business firms and force strategic change
readings: Jensen & Meckling, 'Theory of the Firm: Managerial Behavior, Agency Costs and Ownership Structure' Journal of Financial Economics (1976)
Bebchuk & Fried, Pay Without Performance: The Unfulfilled Promise of Executive Compensation (2004), Ch. 1-4
Monks & Minow, Corporate Governance (6th ed., 2020), Ch. 3 and 5
Brav, Jiang, Partnoy & Thomas, 'Hedge Fund Activism, Corporate Governance, and Firm Performance' Journal of Finance (2008)

lecture_notes.introduction: Who watches the watchers? In a modern publicly traded corporation, shareholders own the firm but managers run it. This separation creates the agency problem: managers may pursue their own interests (larger firms, less risk, more perquisites, shorter work weeks) rather than maximizing shareholder value. Jensen and Meckling's 1976 paper formalized this insight and launched the entire field of modern corporate governance. The mechanisms we use to align manager and shareholder interests — independent boards, incentive compensation, disclosure requirements, takeover threats, activist investors — are institutional answers to this single problem. Corporate governance sits at the heart of corporate strategy because it determines whether strategic decisions actually serve shareholders or merely entrench managers. When Nelson Peltz of Trian Partners took a stake in Procter & Gamble in 2017 and argued the company was underperforming because of organizational complexity and slow brand management, P&G fought a bruising proxy battle and Peltz narrowly won a board seat; two years later Peltz's reforms were credited with improved operating performance. When Bill Ackman's Pershing Square campaigned to break up Target, Sears, or J.C. Penney, outcomes varied but the pattern was the same: activist investors taking concentrated positions and using governance mechanisms to force strategic change. This session teaches you how boards, compensation, and activism interact to shape corporate strategy.
key_concepts: Principal-agent problem: shareholders (principals) hire managers (agents) to run the firm, but managers have private information and their own interests; agency costs include monitoring costs, bonding costs, and residual loss from imperfect alignment

Jensen-Meckling free cash flow theory: cash beyond profitable reinvestment opportunities should be returned to shareholders; managers prefer to retain and invest it in empire-building projects; debt disciplines managers by committing cash to interest payments

Board independence: outside directors without business or family ties to the CEO; independent directors are meant to provide arms-length oversight but effectiveness depends on engagement, information access, and willingness to confront management

Board committees: audit (financial reporting and internal controls), compensation (executive pay), and nominating/governance (director selection and board evaluation); SEC and exchange rules require majority independent members on each

Executive compensation components: base salary, annual bonus (often tied to financial metrics), long-term incentives (stock options, restricted stock, performance share units), retirement and perquisites; pay-for-performance depends on how well metrics align with shareholder value
