questions.question: Apple's App Store generated approximately $24 billion in commissions in 2023 by charging developers 30% (15% for small developers) while allowing iOS users free access to the store. Explain why Apple's pricing structure is economically rational using two-sided market theory. Then explain how this pricing structure creates winner-take-most dynamics and why the EU Digital Markets Act (2022) directly targets it.
questions.answer:
Two-sided market economic rationale: The App Store connects two groups — iOS users and app developers. Cross-side network effects are strong: users value iOS more when the app catalog is larger (more developers attract users), and developers value iOS more when the user base is larger (more users attract developers). Apple's pricing must solve the chicken-and-egg problem of getting both sides on board and then extracting value once network effects are established.

Applying the Rochet-Tirole framework: the side that is more price-sensitive and whose participation is critical to the other side's value should be subsidized, and the other side should bear the revenue load. On iOS:

User side: Highly price sensitive (consumer electronics buyers). Free access to the App Store is essential — charging users to browse apps would dramatically reduce iOS's attractiveness vs Android. Users get the subsidized side: zero cost.

Developer side: Less price-sensitive for the margins Apple charges, because access to iOS's premium user base (higher ARPU than Android globally) justifies the 30% commission for most developers. The App Store's value to a developer is the reach and monetization of Apple's hundreds of millions of paying customers. Developers get the monetized side.

Cross-side contribution: Users need the app ecosystem to exist, developers need the user base to exist, but the user base was established by Apple's hardware sales — Apple had marquee supply (its own apps and launch developer partners) at iPhone launch, which solved the cold-start problem. Subsequent network effects made iOS increasingly valuable to both sides.

Winner-take-most dynamics: Strong two-sided network effects create a tendency toward a small number of dominant platforms. Developers cannot realistically support unlimited platforms (each requires engineering investment, QA, support), so they concentrate on iOS and Android — the two established mobile platforms with critical mass. This creates a duopoly where new mobile OS attempts (Microsoft Windows Phone, Blackberry, Samsung Tizen, Amazon Fire Phone) failed because they could not attract developers without users and could not attract users without developers. The 30% commission sustains because developers have no credible alternative to reach iOS's billion-plus paying users.

EU Digital Markets Act (2022) targeting: The DMA designates 'gatekeeper' platforms that must meet specific obligations, including allowing alternative app stores, sideloading of apps outside official stores, and interoperability with rival services. The DMA's theory is that once a platform has winner-take-most network effects, market forces no longer discipline pricing or behavior, and regulation must substitute. Apple's 30% commission is exactly the kind of gatekeeper rent the DMA aims to reduce by giving developers alternative distribution channels. In response, Apple has reluctantly allowed third-party app stores in the EU (starting with iOS 17.4 in 2024) and reduced commissions in specific contexts. The underlying economics justify Apple's pricing from a platform-strategy perspective, but the same economics justify regulatory intervention from a competition-policy perspective. Both sides are correct given their respective goals.

rubric.key: Correctly explains two-sided pricing logic with Rochet-Tirole framing
Correctly explains winner-take-most dynamics and cold-start problem
Correctly explains why DMA targets the platform pricing structure

questions.question: Synthesize the course. You are advising a private equity fund evaluating Conglomerate X — a $40 billion revenue multi-business firm that owns an aerospace parts business ($15B revenue, 15% EBIT margin, growing 4%), a medical devices business ($12B, 20% margin, growing 7%), a consumer appliances business ($8B, 8% margin, growing 1%), and a financial services business ($5B, mixed results). The combined firm trades at 13x forward earnings, while pure-play aerospace trades at 20x and medical devices at 24x. Propose a restructuring plan that addresses the conglomerate discount, and specify which BA406 frameworks you used.
questions.answer:
Synthesis analysis and restructuring plan:

Diagnosis (applying Rumelt's typology and Porter's tests): Conglomerate X is an unrelated diversifier — aerospace parts, medical devices, consumer appliances, and financial services share no meaningful operational core. The aerospace and medical businesses face different customers, different regulatory regimes, different R&D cycles, different capital intensities. Consumer appliances is low-margin and slow-growing. Financial services is qualitatively different again. Porter's better-off test: there is no evidence the corporate parent improves any of these businesses; each would likely perform better as a focused operator with dedicated management attention and aligned compensation.

Conglomerate discount quantification: The market trades Conglomerate X at 13x forward earnings. Sum-of-parts using pure-play multiples:
- Aerospace: $15B * 15% = $2.25B EBIT; at 20x = $45B
- Medical: $12B * 20% = $2.4B EBIT; at 24x = $57.6B
- Appliances: $8B * 8% = $0.64B EBIT; at 12x mature consumer multiple = $7.7B
- Financial services: $5B revenue, roughly $0.5B profit; at 10x = $5B

Sum-of-parts enterprise value: approximately $115B.

Current conglomerate value at 13x earnings of roughly $5.79B EBIT = $75B enterprise value.

Implied conglomerate discount: approximately ($115B - $75B) / $115B = 35%. This is an unusually large discount, suggesting the market has deeply lost confidence in the parent.

Restructuring plan (applying divestiture toolkit, Haspeslagh-Jemison framework, and Porter):

1. Spin off Medical Devices as an independent public company (tax-free under Section 355 if structured properly). This is the highest-value business and the one with the greatest focus dividend available. Independent management can pursue medical-specific acquisitions, align compensation to medical industry peers, and attract healthcare-focused investors. Expected value unlock: $57.6B vs current allocation of roughly $30B at the conglomerate multiple — unlocking around $27B of shareholder value.

2. Sell Financial Services to a strategic financial acquirer or private equity. This business does not fit any operational parent logic and is dragging multiples. Cash proceeds (estimate $5B) are returned to shareholders or used to pay down debt.

3. Sell Consumer Appliances to a strategic buyer (another appliances maker pursuing consolidation) or a private equity buyer willing to run it for cash flow. This business's low growth and margins make it alien territory for the parent. Estimated proceeds: $7-8B.

4. Retain Aerospace as the remaining core and relist it as a pure-play aerospace supplier. Management and incentive compensation refocused on aerospace KPIs. Expected re-rating from 13x to 18-20x generates roughly $13B of value unlock.

5. Total unlocked value: approximately $45-50B over 18-24 months, roughly 60-65% of current enterprise value. Distribute via combination of share price appreciation, special dividends from divestiture proceeds, and pro rata spin-off shares.

Frameworks used:
- Porter's three tests (1987 HBR) to evaluate each business's fit with the parent
- Rumelt's diversification typology to classify as unrelated
- BCG/GE-McKinsey implicitly in the sum-of-parts analysis
- Divestiture toolkit (spin-off for medical, sale for financial services and appliances)
- Haspeslagh-Jemison integration framework (each divested business gets appropriate standalone or absorption treatment at the acquirer)
- Collis-Montgomery triangle (resources-businesses-organization) to argue that none of the parent's resources meaningfully serve all four businesses
- Jensen-Meckling agency theory to explain why activist/PE pressure is needed to force the restructuring (incumbent management has empire-building incentives to preserve the conglomerate)
- Sirower discipline on any future acquisitions by the post-restructuring independent businesses
- Ghemawat CAGE and Bartlett-Ghoshal frameworks if any of the continuing businesses consider international expansion

Execution note: A private equity fund proposing this plan should either (a) take the whole firm private through a LBO and execute the restructuring outside public market scrutiny, or (b) take a large minority stake and agitate publicly for the plan as an activist investor. Option (a) is cleaner but requires more capital; option (b) uses the governance mechanism to pressure incumbent management to execute. Either way, the core insight is that the conglomerate structure is destroying approximately $40-50B of shareholder value and the restructuring path to capture it is clear.

rubric.key: Correct diagnosis using Rumelt and Porter tests
Correct sum-of-parts calculation quantifying the conglomerate discount
Coherent restructuring plan using appropriate divestiture tools
Explicit integration of at least five course frameworks in the synthesis

major_assignments.title: Diversification Diagnosis and Parenting Advantage Analysis
major_assignments.description: Select one publicly traded multi-business firm from a provided list (including options such as Berkshire Hathaway, 3M, Honeywell, Danaher, Siemens, ABB, Samsung, and Tata Group) and conduct a comprehensive diversification diagnosis. You will classify the firm using Rumelt's typology, apply Porter's three tests and the Collis-Montgomery framework, compute a sum-of-parts valuation against current market cap to quantify any conglomerate discount or premium, and render a reasoned judgment on whether the current portfolio creates or destroys value. The assignment simulates the work of an activist investor or strategy consultant producing a board-level assessment of corporate scope.
objectives: Apply diversification and parenting frameworks to a real multi-business firm
Quantify portfolio value through sum-of-parts analysis against market capitalization
Render a reasoned, evidence-based judgment on corporate scope
Develop the habit of using multiple frameworks together rather than in isolation
requirements: 8-12 page written analysis (not counting appendices) with clear structure: firm overview, diversification classification, Porter test application, sum-of-parts valuation, judgment and recommendation
Sum-of-parts calculation using at least 2 comparable pure-play multiples per business segment, documented in a spreadsheet appendix
At least 10 sources cited including 10-K filings, analyst reports, company press releases, and academic or trade press
Visual: a one-page summary chart showing business segments mapped on Porter's three tests with scoring rationale
Explicit recommendation on whether to divest any segment, acquire any adjacent business, or maintain current portfolio, with reasoning tied to framework outputs
deliverables: Written analysis as PDF (8-12 pages plus appendices)
Sum-of-parts valuation spreadsheet (Excel) with formulas visible
One-page executive summary suitable for a board of directors
15-minute in-class presentation of findings with Q&A from peers acting as board members

grading_rubric.criterion: Accurate application of Rumelt classification and Porter tests
Quality of sum-of-parts analysis (correct methodology, defensible multiples, documented assumptions)
Depth of research (credible sources, specific evidence, engagement with counterarguments)
Clarity and persuasiveness of judgment and recommendation
Quality of written communication, visual presentation, and oral delivery

rubric.key: Exemplary (A)
rubric.value: All frameworks correctly applied with sophisticated interpretation. Sum-of-parts uses multiple credible comparables and explicitly addresses assumption sensitivity. Recommendation is specific, defensible, and tied rigorously to evidence. Writing is polished and presentation is compelling

rubric.key: Proficient (B)
rubric.value: Frameworks applied correctly with minor gaps. Sum-of-parts methodology sound but with some unjustified assumptions. Recommendation reasonable but could be more specific. Writing clear and presentation competent

rubric.key: Developing (C)
rubric.value: Some framework application errors or superficial engagement. Sum-of-parts present but missing key rigor. Recommendation vague or not clearly tied to evidence. Writing and presentation acceptable but not polished

rubric.key: Inadequate (F)
rubric.value: Major framework errors, missing or incorrect sum-of-parts, recommendation unsupported or absent, or writing unclear enough to obscure analysis

major_assignments.title: M&A Synergy Valuation and Integration Plan
major_assignments.description: Select a completed or proposed merger or acquisition from the last five years (minimum deal size $5 billion) and produce both a synergy valuation and a detailed post-merger integration plan. Half of the assignment is quantitative — calculate the premium paid, estimate and document the realistic synergies (cost and revenue), compute the net NPV to the acquirer, and render a verdict on whether the deal was likely value-creating for the acquiring shareholders. The other half is qualitative — apply the Haspeslagh-Jemison integration framework to identify the correct integration approach, build a 100-day plan with specific workstreams, and address cultural, talent, and governance risks. This assignment mirrors the actual diligence and integration planning work done in M&A advisory and corporate development roles.
objectives: Apply synergy valuation with appropriate skepticism and quantitative discipline
Design integration plans that match strategic logic to integration approach
Identify and plan for the most common post-merger pitfalls: culture, talent, and governance
Develop practical skills directly applicable to corporate development and M&A advisory careers
requirements: 15-20 page written analysis plus financial model spreadsheet and 100-day plan
Deal economics section: premium paid, pro forma accretion/dilution, EV/EBITDA at deal vs peers, financing structure
Synergy valuation: specific cost and revenue synergy estimates with source, timing, probability, and discounted present value; explicit comparison of synergy NPV to premium paid

Integration plan: Haspeslagh-Jemison quadrant analysis, detailed 100-day milestones by workstream (commercial, operations, HR, finance, technology), key talent retention plan, culture integration approach, and governance structure
Risk register identifying at least 10 integration risks with mitigation strategies
References to at least 5 academic sources or textbook frameworks and 10 industry/news sources documenting the deal
deliverables: Written analysis as PDF (15-20 pages plus appendices)
Financial model in Excel showing deal economics, synergy calculations, and accretion/dilution
100-day integration plan in presentable visual form (Gantt chart or workstream matrix)
Executive summary memo (2 pages) suitable for an acquirer's CEO and board

grading_rubric.criterion: Rigor of synergy valuation (realism, documentation, NPV discipline)
Quality of integration plan (framework fit, specificity, 100-day rigor)
Identification and management of integration risks (culture, talent, governance)
Analytical judgment on whether the deal was/will be value-creating
Quality of financial modeling, writing, and presentation

rubric.key: Exemplary (A)
rubric.value: Synergy valuation is rigorous with documented source, timing, and probability for each item, discounted appropriately. Integration plan demonstrates sophisticated application of Haspeslagh-Jemison with specific 100-day actions and realistic risk planning. Financial model is clean and verifiable. Judgment is specific and defensible

rubric.key: Proficient (B)
rubric.value: Synergy valuation present and reasonable with minor gaps. Integration plan uses the framework correctly but with some generic elements. Financial model functional. Judgment sound but could be sharper

rubric.key: Developing (C)
rubric.value: Synergy valuation present but with significant methodology gaps. Integration plan applies framework superficially. Financial model has errors. Judgment vague

rubric.key: Inadequate (F)
rubric.value: Missing or seriously flawed synergy valuation, integration plan that does not reflect framework understanding, or major errors in financial model that invalidate conclusions

major_assignments.title: Corporate Strategy Transformation Capstone
major_assignments.description: Select a publicly traded firm undergoing or facing the need for corporate strategy transformation and produce a complete corporate strategy recommendation. The deliverable is a board-ready strategy document that diagnoses the current situation, proposes a future portfolio and operating model, develops a multi-year execution plan, and addresses governance and capital allocation. This capstone integrates every framework from the course: diagnosis using diversification typologies and sum-of-parts, portfolio choice using BCG/GE-McKinsey and real options, M&A and divestiture decisions with synergy discipline, vertical integration analysis using TCE, international strategy using Bartlett-Ghoshal and CAGE, platform considerations where relevant, and governance recommendations. You will present your findings to a panel acting as a board of directors and defend your analysis under questioning.
objectives: Integrate all course frameworks into a coherent strategy recommendation
Demonstrate mastery of diagnostic, design, and execution dimensions of corporate strategy
Develop skills in board-level communication, evidence-based argumentation, and strategic judgment
Prepare for careers in corporate strategy, management consulting, corporate development, or private equity
requirements: 25-30 page strategy document covering diagnosis, future state vision, portfolio decisions, M&A/divestiture moves, organizational implications, governance recommendations, financial projections, and execution roadmap
Integration of at least 8 distinct course frameworks with explicit mapping in an appendix
Financial model showing current performance, projected transformation trajectory, and key financial targets over 5 years
Organizational design recommendations including governance structure, incentive compensation alignment, and cultural change requirements
Risk assessment identifying execution risks, market risks, and stakeholder risks with mitigation strategies
At least 20 sources cited, including 10-K filings, analyst reports, academic literature, and industry press
Alignment with a specific activist or PE thesis where applicable — what change is needed, who would drive it, what resistance is expected
deliverables: Written strategy document as PDF (25-30 pages plus appendices)
Financial model in Excel with scenario analysis
Executive summary (3-5 pages) for board distribution
30-minute oral presentation and defense to faculty panel
Written response to three specific critical questions posed by the instructor after the presentation

grading_rubric.criterion: Quality and rigor of diagnosis (diversification classification, parenting, sum-of-parts)
Coherence of proposed future state and strategic moves
Integration of multiple course frameworks into unified strategy
Rigor of financial analysis and realism of projections
Execution plan quality including organization, governance, and risk
Board presentation quality and defense under questioning

rubric.key: Exemplary (A)
rubric.value: Integrated analysis demonstrates sophisticated command of multiple frameworks. Diagnosis is rigorous, future state is specific and defensible, execution plan is realistic. Financial model is clean. Presentation is compelling and defense under questioning is confident and substantive. Work is of quality that a consulting firm or investment bank would be willing to deliver to a client

rubric.key: Proficient (B)
rubric.value: Analysis uses multiple frameworks competently with minor integration gaps. Diagnosis and future state are sound but could be sharper. Financial model works with minor issues. Presentation is clear and defense adequate

rubric.key: Developing (C)
rubric.value: Analysis applies frameworks but with limited integration. Diagnosis or future state vague. Financial model has significant errors. Presentation and defense show lack of command

rubric.key: Inadequate (F)
rubric.value: Missing rigor in diagnosis, frameworks applied superficially, financial model seriously flawed, or inability to defend the recommendation under questioning

quizzes.description: Five quizzes across the semester covering corporate scope and diversification, vertical and horizontal integration, M&A integration and alliances, governance and restructuring, and real options and platforms

class_participation.description: Case discussion participation, contribution to in-class analyses, and engagement with peers during presentations

major_assignments.description: Three major assignments: diversification diagnosis (10%), M&A synergy and integration plan (15%), and corporate strategy transformation capstone (20%)

midterm_examination.description: Written midterm exam covering weeks 1-8: corporate scope, diversification, portfolio frameworks, core competencies, vertical and horizontal integration, and M&A fundamentals

final_examination.description: Comprehensive written final exam integrating all 15 weeks of material with analytical case questions requiring cross-framework synthesis

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all sessions. Case discussions and in-class analyses depend on student preparation, so unexcused absences harm both the individual student and the class. More than 3 unexcused absences may reduce the participation grade
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day (weekends count). No submissions accepted more than 5 days late except for documented medical or family emergencies. The capstone assignment has a firm due date due to presentation scheduling
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion of frameworks, cases, and approaches with peers is encouraged. All written analysis, financial modeling, and presentations must be the student's individual work. Collaborative assignments will be explicitly identified in the syllabus
course_policies.academic_integrity: All submitted work must be the student's own. Citing sources is mandatory; uncited use of another's ideas constitutes plagiarism. Use of AI tools for drafting is permitted only where explicitly allowed and must be disclosed; AI tools may not be used to generate the core analysis that the assignment is evaluating
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need Excel for financial modeling, access to a major financial database (Bloomberg, S&P Capital IQ, or FactSet through the university library), access to academic databases (JSTOR, Business Source Complete), and presentation software. Laptops are permitted in class but case discussion requires active engagement, not passive note-taking
